Editing files in other directories requires approval. Network access is restricted.\n# Escalation Requests\n\nCommands are run outside the sandbox if they are approved by the user, or match an existing rule that allows it to run unrestricted. The command string is split into independent command segments at shell control operators, including but not limited to:\n\n- Pipes: |\n- Logical operators: &&, ||\n- Command separators: ;\n- Subshell boundaries: (...), $(...)\n\nEach resulting segment is evaluated independently for sandbox restrictions and approval requirements.\n\nExample:\n\ngit pull | tee output.txt\n\nThis is treated as two command segments:\n\n[\"git\", \"pull\"]\n\n[\"tee\", \"output.txt\"]\n\nCommands that use more advanced shell features like redirection (>, >>, <), substitutions ($(...), ...), environment variables (FOO=bar), or wildcard patterns (*, ?) will not be evaluated against rules, to limit the scope of what an approved rule allows.\n\n## How to request escalation\n\nIMPORTANT: To request approval to execute a command that will require escalated privileges:\n\n- Provide the `sandbox_permissions` parameter with the value `\"require_escalated\"`\n- Include a short question asking the user if they want to allow the action in `justification` parameter. e.g. \"Do you want to download and install dependencies for this project?\"\n- Optionally suggest a `prefix_rule` - this will be shown to the user with an option to persist the rule approval for future sessions.\n\nIf you run a command that is important to solving the user's query, but it fails because of sandboxing or with a likely sandbox-related network error (for example DNS/host resolution, registry/index access, or dependency download failure), rerun the command with \"require_escalated\". stats[\"ema_fast\"] = (\n                self.alpha_fast * percent_done +\n                (1.0 - self.alpha_fast) * stats[\"ema_fast\"]\n            )\n            stats[\"ema_slow\"] = (\n                self.alpha_slow * percent_done +\n                (1.0 - self.alpha_slow) * stats[\"ema_slow\"]\n            )\n            stats[\"solve_ema\"] = (\n                self.alpha_fast * (1.0 if percent_done >= 0.999 else 0.0) +\n                (1.0 - self.alpha_fast) * stats[\"solve_ema\"]\n            )\n\n        stats[\"history\"].append(percent_done)\n        if len(stats[\"history\"]) > self.stats_window:\n            stats[\"history\"].pop(0)\n        stats[\"count\"] += 1\n        self._compute_probs()\n\n    def get_probs(self):\n        return self._probs.tolist()\n\n    def get_env_metrics(self, env_idx):\n        stats = self._stats[env_idx]\n        hist = np.asarray(stats[\"history\"], dtype=np.float32)\n\n        median = float(np.median(hist)) if len(hist) else np.nan\n        std = float(np.std(hist)) if len(hist) else np.nan\n\n        if len(hist) >= 10:\n            best = float(np.percentile(hist, 90))\n            current = max(0.0, median - self.std_penalty * std)\n            rel_skill = 0.0 if best < 0.3 else current / (best + 1e-6)\n        else:\n            rel_skill = np.nan\n\n        stage = self._anchor\n        if stage + 1 < self.num_envs:\n            denom = self._probs[stage] + self._probs[stage + 1]\n            mix_progress = float(self._probs[stage + 1] / denom) if denom > 0 else 0.0\n        else:\n            mix_progress = 0.0\n\n        return {\n            \"rel_skill\": rel_skill,\n            \"_mix_progress\": mix_progress,\n            \"_stage\": stage,\n            \"median_history\": median,\n            \"std_history\": std,\n        }\n\n    def get_stats(self):\n        return [\n            {\n                \"count\": stats[\"count\"],\n                \"ema_fast\": stats[\"ema_fast\"],\n                \"ema_slow\": stats[\"ema_slow\"],\n                \"solve_ema\": stats[\"solve_ema\"],\n            }\n            for stats in self._stats\n        ]\n\n\nclass EnvCurriculumAdaptiveDistributed:\n    def __init__(self, env_configs, curriculum_manager):\n        self.env_configs = env_configs\n        self.curriculum_manager = curriculum_manager\n        self._pos_env = 0\n        self.env = self.env_configs[0].create_env()\n\n    def __getattr__(self, name):\n        if name == \"cur_env\":\n            return self._pos_env\n        if name == \"reset\":\n            return self._reset\n        return getattr(self.env, name)\n\n    def report_outcome(self, percent_done):\n        if self.curriculum_manager is not None:\n            ray.get(self.curriculum_manager.report_outcome.remote(self._pos_env, percent_done))\n\n    def get_curriculum_metrics(self):\n        if self.curriculum_manager is None:\n            return {}\n ray.get(self.curriculum_manager.sample_env.remote())\n        else:\n            next_env = np.random.randint(len(self.env_configs))\n        if next_env != self._pos_env:\n            self._pos_env = next_env\n            self.env = self.env_configs[self._pos_env].create_env()\n        return self.env.reset()\n    \r\n# In Multiagent every worker goes through curriculum\r\nclass self._reset\r\n        return getattr(self.env, name)\r\n\r\n\r\n    def _start_next(self):\r\n        if self._pos_env + 1 < len(self.env_configs): # keep launching last env_episode\r\n            self._pos_env += 1\r\n        self._cur_env_episode = 0\r\n        return self.env_configs[self._pos_env].create_env()\r\n\r\n\r\n    def _reset(self):\r\n        if self._cur_env_episode == checkpoint['actor_state'],\r\n                checkpoint['critic_state'],\r\n                checkpoint['target_actor_state']\r\n            ))\r\n else:\r\n            print(f\"No checkpoint found at {checkpoint_path}, starting fresh.\")\r\n            \r\n    # TODO different updates for target_actor/actor\r\n    def _calc_loss(self, state, action, old_log_prob, reward, next_state, done, gae, neighbours_states, actual_len):\r\n        state_values = self.controller.critic_net(state).squeeze(1)\r\n        with torch.no_grad():\r\n            next_state_values = self.controller.critic_net(next_state).squeeze(1)\r\n\r\n        logits = self.controller._make_logits(state, neighbours_states)\r\n\r\n        action_distribution = Categorical(logits=logits)\r\n        new_log_prob = action_distribution.log_prob(action)\r\n\r\n        critic_loss = value_loss_with_IS(state_values, next_state_values, new_log_prob, old_log_prob, reward, done, self.gamma, actual_len)\r\n        actor_loss = policy_loss(gae, new_log_prob, old_log_prob, self.clip_eps)\r\n        entropy_loss = -action_distribution.entropy().mean()\r\n\r\n        return critic_loss * self.value_loss_coeff + actor_loss + entropy_loss * self.entropy_coeff\r\n\r\n\r\n    def _optimize(self, rollout_dict):\r\n        # all agents rollouts combined\r\n        rollouts = [rollout for rollout in rollout_dict.values() if not rollout.is_empty()]\r\n        if not rollouts:\r\n            return\r\n        combined_rollout = PPORollout.combine_rollouts(rollouts)\r\n        self.rollouts_buffer.append(combined_rollout)\r\n\r\n        # optimize by batch of several rollouts\r\n        if len(self.rollouts_buffer) != self.rollouts_sample:\r\n            return\r\n\r\n        combined_rollout = PPORollout.combine_rollouts(self.rollouts_buffer)\r\n        self.rollouts_buffer.clear()\r\n\r\n        state, action, log_prob, reward, next_state, done, neighbours_states, actual_len = combined_rollout.unzip_transitions(self.device)\r\n        gae = combined_rollout.gae.to(self.device)\r\n\r\n        for _ in Only as a last resort, keep manually lowering thresholds run by run.\n\nIf you do want a difficulty-aware rule later, a simple version is:\n- early envs: `unlock_rel_skill ~ 0.88-0.90`\n- mid envs: `~ 0.85-0.87`\n- late envs: `~ 0.82-0.85`\n\nThat is more defensible than repeatedly editing one hardcoded threshold.\n\nSo my recommendation is:\n- create `run11`\n- resume the PPO weights from `run10`\n- add curriculum save/restore for future resumes\n- slightly relax the unlock rule once\n- see whether the run can now push past the current env-4/env-5 boundary before making it more complicated\n\nIf you want, I can patch that now so `run11`:\n- resumes from `run10/checkpoint.pth`\n- saves/restores adaptive curriculum state\n- uses a slightly relaxed unlock gate.","completed_at":1776715070,"duration_ms":39424}}

{"timestamp":"2026-04-20T20:06:11.997Z","type":"response_item","payload":{"type":"message","role":"user","content":[{"type":"input_text","text":"Our solution is based on Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) (Schulman et al., 2017)\nenhanced with between-agent communication. Communication has been shown to help\nagents to solve simple coordination problems (Sukhbaatar et al., 2016; Das et al., 2019), and\nour solution extends this result to the complex Flatland environment.\nRewards For each agent, its reward is defined as 0.01 × ∆d − 5 × is_deadlocked + 10 ×\nhas_finished, where ∆d is the difference between the minimal distances to the destination\npoint from its previous and current positions, and is_deadlocked and has_finished are\nthe respective indicators of whether the agent is deadlocked or has successfully completed\nthe episode.\nArchitecture In our solution, the agents are based on the actor-critic framework. The\nactor makes decisions whenever encountering a switch, based on features from three sources\n(see Figure G.1 in Appendix G):\nFirst, it receives information about the agent itself, such as its position on the map and\nindicators of whether it is deadlocked or malfunctioning. At the beginning of the episode,\neach agent is also assigned with a handle, a random number uniformly sampled from [0, 1]\nwhich is supposed to represent priority. This information is processed with a fully-connected\nnetwork (denoted as “Common Features Net”).\nSecond, the actor observes the most relevant part of the map, encoded as the edges of\na tree with a depth limited to 3. The features of each edge include its length, distance to\nthe destination from its nodes, as well as information about other agents, including their\npositions and handles. The features of different edges are combined with a recursive network\n(denoted as “Tree Features Net”), albeit replacing it with a fully-connected network produces\nsimilar performance.\nThird, prior to action selection, the actor receives a set of floating-point vector messages\ngenerated by the neighbouring agents, i.e. the agents observed on the tree. Similarly to\nATOC (Jiang and Lu, 2018), the messages are processed with self-attention layers (Vaswani\net al., 2017).\nAll components are trained end-to-end using PPO. The agents share experiences and\nnetwork parameters. The architecture of the critic is similar to that of the actor, except the\ncritic does not condition on communication signals. For a more complete description of the\narchitecture and the features, please refer to our code.8\nDeparture Schedule To limit the maximum occupancy, we introduce a departure sched-\nule. After pre-training agents without the schedule, we train a supervised classifier that\ndetermines the chances that each agent reaches its destination. Then, we only allow each\ntrain to start moving if the predicted probability exceeds a threshold, set to 0.92 at the start\nof the episode, and gradually lowered over time.\nWe found that a carefully tuned PPO with simple heuristics for launching agents can achieve\nresults comparable to simple algorithms from the operations research field while being quite\nfast in execution. Among the modifications that we applied to the classic PPO algorithm,\ncommunication was by far the most impactful. It significantly increased the arrival rate\nwithout compromising scalability. One particular limitation of our solution is that we train\nthe agents in relatively small environments. Consequently, their performance may degrade\nas the environment scales up\n\nI found the papaer excepr to this particular model with the particular problem. {"timestamp":"2026-04-20T20:06:11.998Z","type":"event_msg","payload":{"type":"user_message","message":"Our solution is based on Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) (Schulman et al., 2017)\nenhanced with between-agent communication. Communication has been shown to help\nagents to solve simple coordination problems (Sukhbaatar et al., 2016; Das et al., 2019), and\nour solution extends this result to the complex Flatland environment.\nRewards For each agent, its reward is defined as 0.01 × ∆d − 5 × is_deadlocked + 10 ×\nhas_finished, where ∆d is the difference between the minimal distances to the destination\npoint from its previous and current positions, and is_deadlocked and has_finished are\nthe respective indicators of whether the agent is deadlocked or has successfully completed\nthe episode.\nArchitecture In our solution, the agents are based on the actor-critic framework. The\nactor makes decisions whenever encountering a switch, based on features from three sources\n(see Figure G.1 in Appendix G):\nFirst, it receives information about the agent itself, such as its position on the map and\nindicators of whether it is deadlocked or malfunctioning. At the beginning of the episode,\neach agent is also assigned with a handle, a random number uniformly sampled from [0, 1]\nwhich is supposed to represent priority. This information is processed with a fully-connected\nnetwork (denoted as “Common Features Net”).\nSecond, the actor observes the most relevant part of the map, encoded as the edges of\na tree with a depth limited to 3. The features of each edge include its length, distance to\nthe destination from its nodes, as well as information about other agents, including their\npositions and handles. The features of different edges are combined with a recursive network\n(denoted as “Tree Features Net”), albeit replacing it with a fully-connected network produces\nsimilar performance.\nThird, prior to action selection, the actor receives a set of floating-point vector messages\ngenerated by the neighbouring agents, i.e. the agents observed on the tree. Similarly to\nATOC (Jiang and Lu, 2018), the messages are processed with self-attention layers (Vaswani\net al., 2017).\nAll components are trained end-to-end using PPO. The agents share experiences and\nnetwork parameters. The architecture of the critic is similar to that of the actor, except the\ncritic does not condition on communication signals. For a more complete description of the\narchitecture and the features, please refer to our code.8\nDeparture Schedule To limit the maximum occupancy, we introduce a departure sched-\nule. After pre-training agents without the schedule, we train a supervised classifier that\ndetermines the chances that each agent reaches its destination. Then, we only allow each\ntrain to start moving if the predicted probability exceeds a threshold, set to 0.92 at the start\nof the episode, and gradually lowered over time.\nWe found that a carefully tuned PPO with simple heuristics for launching agents can achieve\nresults comparable to simple algorithms from the operations research field while being quite\nfast in execution. Among the modifications that we applied to the classic PPO algorithm,\ncommunication was by far the most impactful. It significantly increased the arrival rate\nwithout compromising scalability. One particular limitation of our solution is that we train\nthe agents in relatively small environments. Consequently, their performance may degrade\nas the environment scales up\n\nI found the papaer excepr to this particular model with the particular problem. _calc_gae(self, state, next_state, reward, done, actual_len):\r\n        # moving this code to worker saves learner time\r\n        with torch.no_grad():\r\n            state_values = self.controller.critic_net(state).squeeze(1)\r\n            next_values = torch.cat([state_values[1:], self.controller.critic_net(next_state[-1:]).squeeze(1)])\r\n            expected_state_values = {"timestamp":"2026-04-20T20:11:05.427Z","type":"event_msg","payload":{"type":"agent_message","message":"A limiter can help, but only as a scaffold. If you keep it on permanently, you train the policy on an easier traffic regime than the one you ultimately care about.\n\nIn your code, the gate works by withholding observations until a train is “ready” [SimpleObservation.py](/home/mxw4k/Downloads/jbr-flatland-new(1)/jbr-flatland-new/env/observations/SimpleObservation.py:62). Right now it is effectively off because `window_size_generator=LinearOnAgentNumberSizeGenerator(0.0, 10**10)` makes the judge allow essentially unlimited departures [train.py](/home/mxw4k/Downloads/jbr-flatland-new(1)/jbr-flatland-new/train.py:51).\n\nFor your goal, I would not turn the learned judge back on. I would only consider a simple deterministic limiter if the late envs keep collapsing under full occupancy. The best version is not “limit forever,” but “limit early, then anneal away”:\n- Start the hardest buckets with a cap on active trains.\n- Increase that cap over training or over episode time.\n- End up at full release, because that is the behavior you actually need on the last env.\n\nThat gives you two benefits:\n- It reduces the coordination shock on dense envs.\n- It does not trap the policy in a low-density regime.\n\nWhat I would do in order:\n1. First try your current `run11` change only: resume weights and relax the curriculum unlock slightly.\n2. If it still stalls on late buckets, add a very simple launch cap, not the dormant judge.\n3. Make the cap stage-aware or time-aware, so late training still sees all trains.\n\nConcretely, a reasonable first limiter would be something like:\n- For harder envs only, allow at most `max(8, int(0.25 * n_agents))` active trains at once.\n- Increase that toward `n_agents` as the episode progresses, or as curriculum stage advances.\n\nI would not use a fixed small cap on the final env. That usually improves `percent_done` by avoiding the real problem rather than solving it.\n\nSo: yes, limiting trains may help, but only as temporary curriculum support. For your objective, full release should remain the end state. If you want, I can patch in a simple non-learned active-train cap with an annealing schedule instead of the judge.","phase":"final_answer","memory_citation":null}}

I would only consider a simple deterministic limiter if the late envs keep collapsing under full occupancy. The best version is not “limit forever,” but “limit early, then anneal away”:\n- Start the hardest buckets with a cap on active trains.\n- Increase that cap over training or over episode time.\n- End up at full release, because that is the behavior you actually need on the last env.\n\nThat gives you two benefits:\n- It reduces the coordination shock on dense envs.\n- It does not trap the policy in a low-density regime.\n\nWhat I would do in order:\n1. First try your current `run11` change only: resume weights and relax the curriculum unlock slightly.\n2. If it still stalls on late buckets, add a very simple launch cap, not the dormant judge.\n3. Make the cap stage-aware or time-aware, so late training still sees all trains.\n\nConcretely, a reasonable first limiter would be something like:\n- For harder envs only, allow at most `max(8, int(0.25 * n_agents))` active trains at once.\n- Increase that toward `n_agents` as the episode progresses, or as curriculum stage advances.\n\nI would not use a fixed small cap on the final env. That usually improves `percent_done` by avoiding the real problem rather than solving it.\n\nSo: yes, limiting trains may help, but only as temporary curriculum support. Right now it is effectively off because `window_size_generator=LinearOnAgentNumberSizeGenerator(0.0, 10**10)` makes the judge allow essentially unlimited departures [train.py](/home/mxw4k/Downloads/jbr-flatland-new(1)/jbr-flatland-new/train.py:51).\n\nFor your goal, I would not turn the learned judge back on. I would only consider a simple deterministic limiter if the late envs keep collapsing under full occupancy. The best version is not “limit forever,” but “limit early, then anneal away”:\n- Start the hardest buckets with a cap on active trains.\n- Increase that cap over training or over episode time.\n- End up at full release, because that is the behavior you actually need on the last env.\n\nThat gives you two benefits:\n- It reduces the coordination shock on dense envs.\n- It does not trap the policy in a low-density regime.\n\nWhat I would do in order:\n1. First try your current `run11` change only: resume weights and relax the curriculum unlock slightly.\n2. If it still stalls on late buckets, add a very simple launch cap, not the dormant judge.\n3. Make the cap stage-aware or time-aware, so late training still sees all trains.\n\nConcretely, a reasonable first limiter would be something like:\n- For harder envs only, allow at most `max(8, int(0.25 * n_agents))` active trains at once.\n- Increase that toward `n_agents` as the episode progresses, or as curriculum stage advances.\n\nI would not use a fixed small cap on the final env. That usually improves `percent_done` by avoiding the real problem rather than solving it.\n\nSo: yes, limiting trains may help, but only as temporary curriculum support. For your objective, full release should remain the end state. If you want, I can patch in a simple non-learned active-train cap with an annealing schedule instead of the judge.","completed_at":1776715865,"duration_ms":23093}}

{"timestamp":"2026-04-20T20:13:32.344Z","type":"event_msg","payload":{"type":"agent_message","message":"For this setup, the most important plots are the curriculum ones, not raw `percent_done`.\n\nFocus on these first:\n\n- `curriculum_stage`\n  This tells you whether training is actually advancing through the curriculum. If this is flat for a long time, that is the real sign of being stuck.\n\n- `curriculum_rel_skill`\n  This is the key unlock signal. In your current config, the anchor only advances when `rel_skill >= 0.88` in `run11`. If this sits below that, the anchor will not move.\n\n- `curriculum_median_history`\n  This tells you the typical recent performance on each env bucket. It is much more useful than the noisy per-episode `percent_done`.\n\n- `curriculum_std_history`\n  This explains why `rel_skill` may stay low even when `median_history` looks decent. High variance is what hurts you here.\n\n- `curriculum_mix_progress`\n  This tells you whether the sampler is leaning more into the next frontier env or falling back toward the current anchor.\n\n`percent_done` is still useful, but only if you read it per env. As a mixed curriculum plot, it is easy to misread.\n\nWhat I would actually watch during training:\n\n1. `curriculum_stage`\n2. `curriculum_rel_skill` for the current anchor env\n3. `curriculum_median_history` and `curriculum_std_history` for the anchor and next env\n4. `percent_done` only as a secondary sanity check\n\nWhat I would mostly ignore:\n\n- `reward` and `shaped_reward`\n  They can move for reasons that do not correspond to actual curriculum progress.\n- `judge_loss` / `judge_threshold`\n  In your current branch, the judge training is commented out, so these are not very informative.\n\nIf you want one simple rule:\n- If `percent_done` is flat but `rel_skill` is rising, keep training.\n- If `stage` is flat and `rel_skill` is flat below threshold, you are genuinely stalled.\n- If `median_history` is fine but `std_history` stays high, the issue is instability, not lack of ability.\n\nOne thing missing from your current plots is per-env sample count or sampling probability. That would make diagnosis much easier. If you want, I can add logging for:\n- env sample counts\n- current curriculum probabilities\n- current anchor env index as a dedicated scalar","phase":"final_answer","memory_citation":null}}

temperature=0.25,\n        progress_weight=0.6,\n        underperformance_weight=0.3,\n        exploration_weight=0.1,\n        unlock_threshold=0.30,\n        unlock_min_count=50,\n        frontier_lookahead=1,\n        fallback_span=1,\n        unlock_rel_skill=0.90,\n        unlock_median_floor=0.45,\n    ):\n        self.num_envs = num_envs\n        self.alpha_fast = alpha_fast\n        self.alpha_slow = alpha_slow\n        self.stats_window = stats_window\n        self.std_penalty = std_penalty\n        self.min_prob = min_prob\n        self.temperature = temperature\n        self.progress_weight = progress_weight\n        self.underperformance_weight = underperformance_weight\n        self.exploration_weight = exploration_weight\n        self.unlock_threshold = unlock_threshold\n        self.unlock_min_count = unlock_min_count\n        self.frontier_lookahead = frontier_lookahead\n        self.fallback_span = fallback_span\n        self.unlock_rel_skill = unlock_rel_skill\n        self.unlock_median_floor = unlock_median_floor\n\n        self._stats = [\n            {\n                \"count\": 0,\n                \"ema_fast\": 0.0,\n                \"ema_slow\": 0.0,\n                \"solve_ema\": 0.0,\n                \"history\": [],\n            }\n            for _ in range(num_envs)\n        ]\n        self._probs = np.ones(num_envs, dtype=np.float64) / float(num_envs)\n        self._anchor = 0\n\n    def _update_anchor(self):\n        # Keep frontier progression monotonic. Flatland is noisy enough that\n        # recomputing unlockability from scratch causes the anchor to collapse\n        # backward after temporary dips on already-solved buckets.\n        anchor = self._anchor\n        while anchor + 1 < self.num_envs:\n            stats = self._stats[anchor]\n            hist = np.asarray(stats[\"history\"], dtype=np.float32)\n            median = float(np.median(hist)) if len(hist) else 0.0\n            if len(hist) >= 10:\n                best = float(np.percentile(hist, 90))\n                std = float(np.std(hist))\n                current = max(0.0, median - self.std_penalty * std)\n                rel_skill = 0.0 if best < 0.3 else current / (best + 1e-6)\n            else:\n                rel_skill = 0.0\n            if (\n                stats[\"count\"] >= self.unlock_min_count and\n                stats[\"ema_slow\"] >= self.unlock_threshold and\n                median >= self.unlock_median_floor and\n                rel_skill >= self.unlock_rel_skill\n            ):\n                anchor += 1\n            else:\n                break\n        self._anchor = anchor\n\n    def _compute_probs(self):\n        scores = np.zeros(self.num_envs, dtype=np.float64)\n        self._update_anchor()\n\n        min_allowed = max(0, self._anchor - self.fallback_span)\n        max_allowed = min(self.num_envs - 1, self._anchor + self.frontier_lookahead)\n        allowed_mask = np.zeros(self.num_envs, dtype=bool)\n        allowed_mask[min_allowed:max_allowed + 1] = True\n\n        for idx, stats in enumerate(self._stats):\n            if not allowed_mask[idx]:\n                scores[idx] = -np.inf\n                continue\n\n            count = stats[\"count\"]\n            if count == 0:\n                progress = 0.0\n                solve_ema = 0.0\n            else:\n                progress = stats[\"ema_fast\"] - stats[\"ema_slow\"]\n                solve_ema = stats[\"solve_ema\"]\n\n            # Prefer buckets with ongoing learning progress, but keep pressure\n            # on buckets that are not solved yet and a little exploration.\n            underperformance = 1.0 - solve_ema\n            exploration = 1.0 / np.sqrt(count + 1.0)\n\n            scores[idx] = (\n                self.progress_weight * progress +\n                self.underperformance_weight * underperformance +\n                self.exploration_weight * exploration\n            )\n\n        finite_scores = scores[allowed_mask]\n        scores[allowed_mask] -= np.max(finite_scores)\n        weights = np.exp(scores / max(self.temperature, 1e-6))\n        weights[~allowed_mask] = 0.0\n        probs = weights / np.sum(weights)\n\n        # Keep a floor on buckets within the active frontier to avoid forgetting.\n        probs[allowed_mask] = np.maximum(probs[allowed_mask], self.min_prob)\n        probs[~allowed_mask] = 0.0\n        probs /= np.sum(probs)\n        self._probs = probs\n\n    def sample_env(self):\n        return int(np.random.choice(self.num_envs, p=self._probs))\n\n    def report_outcome(self, env_idx, percent_done):\n        stats = self._stats[env_idx]\n\n        if stats[\"count\"] == 0:\n            stats[\"ema_fast\"] = percent_done\n            stats[\"ema_slow\"] = percent_done\n            stats[\"solve_ema\"] = 1.0 if percent_done >= 0.999 else 0.0\n        else:\n            stats[\"ema_fast\"] = (\n                self.alpha_fast * percent_done +\n                (1.0 - self.alpha_fast) * stats[\"ema_fast\"]\n            )\n            stats[\"ema_slow\"] = (\n                self.alpha_slow * percent_done +\n                (1.0 - self.alpha_slow) * stats[\"ema_slow\"]\n            )\n            stats[\"solve_ema\"] = (\n                self.alpha_fast * (1.0 if percent_done >= 0.999 else 0.0) +\n                (1.0 - self.alpha_fast) * stats[\"solve_ema\"]\n            )\n\n        stats[\"history\"].append(percent_done)\n        if len(stats[\"history\"]) > self.stats_window:\n            stats[\"history\"].pop(0)\n        stats[\"count\"] += 1\n        self._compute_probs()\n\n    def get_probs(self):\n        return self._probs.tolist()\n\n    def get_env_metrics(self, env_idx):\n        stats = self._stats[env_idx]\n        hist = np.asarray(stats[\"history\"], dtype=np.float32)\n\n        median = float(np.median(hist)) if len(hist) else np.nan\n        std = float(np.std(hist)) if len(hist) else np.nan\n\n        if len(hist) >= 10:\n            best = float(np.percentile(hist, 90))\n            current = max(0.0, median - self.std_penalty * std)\n            rel_skill = 0.0 if best < 0.3 else current / (best + 1e-6)\n        else:\n            rel_skill = np.nan\n\n        stage = self._anchor\n        if stage + 1 < self.num_envs:\n            denom = self._probs[stage] + self._probs[stage + 1]\n            mix_progress = float(self._probs[stage + 1] / denom) if denom > 0 else 0.0\n        else:\n","exit_code":0,"duration":{"secs":0,"nanos":113393799},"formatted_output":"","status":"completed"}}

Keep frontier progression monotonic. Flatland is noisy enough that\n        # recomputing unlockability from scratch causes the anchor to collapse\n        # backward after temporary dips on already-solved buckets.\n        anchor = self._anchor\n        while anchor + 1 < self.num_envs:\n            stats = self._stats[anchor]\n            hist = np.asarray(stats[\"history\"], dtype=np.float32)\n            median = float(np.median(hist)) if len(hist) else 0.0\n            if len(hist) >= 10:\n                best = float(np.percentile(hist, 90))\n                std = float(np.std(hist))\n                current = max(0.0, median - self.std_penalty * std)\n                rel_skill = 0.0 if best < 0.3 else current / (best + 1e-6)\n            else:\n                rel_skill = 0.0\n            if (\n                stats[\"count\"] >= self.unlock_min_count and\n                stats[\"ema_slow\"] >= self.unlock_threshold and\n                median >= self.unlock_median_floor and\n                rel_skill >= self.unlock_rel_skill\n            ):\n                anchor += 1\n            else:\n                break\n        self._anchor = anchor\n\n    def _compute_probs(self):\n        scores = np.zeros(self.num_envs, dtype=np.float64)\n        self._update_anchor()\n\n        min_allowed = max(0, self._anchor - self.fallback_span)\n        max_allowed = min(self.num_envs - 1, self._anchor + self.frontier_lookahead)\n        allowed_mask = np.zeros(self.num_envs, dtype=bool)\n        allowed_mask[min_allowed:max_allowed + 1] = True\n\n        for idx, stats in enumerate(self._stats):\n            if not allowed_mask[idx]:\n                scores[idx] = -np.inf\n                continue\n\n            count = stats[\"count\"]\n            if count == 0:\n                progress = 0.0\n                solve_ema = 0.0\n            else:\n                progress = stats[\"ema_fast\"] - stats[\"ema_slow\"]\n                solve_ema = stats[\"solve_ema\"]\n\n            # Prefer buckets with ongoing learning progress, but keep pressure\n            # on buckets that are not solved yet and a little exploration.\n            underperformance = 1.0 - solve_ema\n            exploration = 1.0 / np.sqrt(count + 1.0)\n\n            scores[idx] = (\n                self.progress_weight * progress +\n                self.underperformance_weight * underperformance +\n                self.exploration_weight * exploration\n            )\n\n        finite_scores = scores[allowed_mask]\n        scores[allowed_mask] -= np.max(finite_scores)\n        weights = np.exp(scores / max(self.temperature, 1e-6))\n        weights[~allowed_mask] = 0.0\n        probs = weights / np.sum(weights)\n\n        # Keep a floor on buckets within the active frontier to avoid forgetting.\n        probs[allowed_mask] = np.maximum(probs[allowed_mask], self.min_prob)\n        probs[~allowed_mask] = 0.0\n        probs /= np.sum(probs)\n        self._probs = probs\n\n    def sample_env(self):\n        return int(np.random.choice(self.num_envs, p=self._probs))\n\n    def report_outcome(self, env_idx, percent_done):\n        stats = self._stats[env_idx]\n\n        if stats[\"count\"] == 0:\n            stats[\"ema_fast\"] = percent_done\n            stats[\"ema_slow\"] = percent_done\n            stats[\"solve_ema\"] = 1.0 if percent_done >= 0.999 else 0.0\n        else:\n            stats[\"ema_fast\"] = (\n                self.alpha_fast * percent_done +\n                (1.0 - self.alpha_fast) * stats[\"ema_fast\"]\n            )\n            stats[\"ema_slow\"] = (\n                self.alpha_slow * percent_done +\n                (1.0 - self.alpha_slow) * stats[\"ema_slow\"]\n            )\n            stats[\"solve_ema\"] = (\n                self.alpha_fast * (1.0 if percent_done >= 0.999 else 0.0) +\n                (1.0 - self.alpha_fast) * stats[\"solve_ema\"]\n            )\n\n        stats[\"history\"].append(percent_done)\n        if len(stats[\"history\"]) > self.stats_window:\n            stats[\"history\"].pop(0)\n        stats[\"count\"] += 1\n        self._compute_probs()\n\n    def get_probs(self):\n        return self._probs.tolist()\n\n    def get_env_metrics(self, env_idx):\n        stats = self._stats[env_idx]\n        hist = np.asarray(stats[\"history\"], dtype=np.float32)\n\n        median = float(np.median(hist)) if len(hist) else np.nan\n        std = float(np.std(hist)) if len(hist) else np.nan\n\n        if len(hist) >= 10:\n            best = float(np.percentile(hist, 90))\n            current = max(0.0, median - Flatland is noisy enough that\n   317\t        # recomputing unlockability from scratch causes the anchor to collapse\n   318\t        # backward after temporary dips on already-solved buckets.\n   319\t        anchor = self._anchor\n   320\t        while anchor + 1 < self.num_envs:\n   321\t            stats = self._stats[anchor]\n   322\t            hist = np.asarray(stats[\"history\"], dtype=np.float32)\n   323\t            median = float(np.median(hist)) if len(hist) else 0.0\n   324\t            if len(hist) >= 10:\n   325\t                best = float(np.percentile(hist, 90))\n   326\t                std = float(np.std(hist))\n   327\t                current = max(0.0, median - self.std_penalty * std)\n   328\t                rel_skill = 0.0 if best < 0.3 else current / (best + 1e-6)\n   329\t            else:\n   330\t                rel_skill = 0.0\n   331\t            if (\n   332\t                stats[\"count\"] >= self.unlock_min_count and\n   333\t                stats[\"ema_slow\"] >= self.unlock_threshold and\n   334\t                median >= self.unlock_median_floor and\n   335\t                rel_skill >= self._required_rel_skill(anchor)\n   336\t            ):\n   337\t                anchor += 1\n   338\t            else:\n   339\t                break\n   340\t        self._anchor = anchor\n"}}

A true capacity problem usually looks like:\n- early curriculum also underfits badly\n- training and validation-like metrics both saturate low\n- stronger PPO updates still cannot move the harder buckets at all\n\nWhat you have looks more like:\n- earlier buckets learn\n- env `5` is repeatedly reached\n- env `5/6` get sampled, but do not consolidate\n- curriculum tweaks change exposure more than actual skill\n\nThat pattern is often caused by training dynamics, not raw model size:\n- updates too infrequent\n- PPO too conservative\n- reward too myopic for dense coordination\n- observations not expressive enough for late congestion\n- occupancy regime too hard\n\nSo yes, net size could be part of it, but I would rank likely causes roughly like this:\n1. training/update efficiency\n2. reward/credit assignment on dense maps\n3. observation limitations\n4. occupancy/release regime\n5. model capacity\n\nA bigger net can even make this worse if optimization is already weak.\n\nThe clean way to test the “net too small” hypothesis is not to guess. A true capacity problem usually looks like:\n- early curriculum also underfits badly\n- training and validation-like metrics both saturate low\n- stronger PPO updates still cannot move the harder buckets at all\n\nWhat you have looks more like:\n- earlier buckets learn\n- env `5` is repeatedly reached\n- env `5/6` get sampled, but do not consolidate\n- curriculum tweaks change exposure more than actual skill\n\nThat pattern is often caused by training dynamics, not raw model size:\n- updates too infrequent\n- PPO too conservative\n- reward too myopic for dense coordination\n- observations not expressive enough for late congestion\n- occupancy regime too hard\n\nSo yes, net size could be part of it, but I would rank likely causes roughly like this:\n1. training/update efficiency\n2. reward/credit assignment on dense maps\n3. observation limitations\n4. occupancy/release regime\n5. model capacity\n\nA bigger net can even make this worse if optimization is already weak.\n\nThe clean way to test the “net too small” hypothesis is not to guess. Do one controlled experiment:\n- keep curriculum fixed\n- improve PPO update frequency first\n- then run one larger-net variant only\n\nFor example:\n- current: `[512, 256]`\n- test: `[768, 384]` or `[1024, 512]`\n\nIf that bigger model does not clearly improve env `5/6` under the same training setup, then capacity was not the bottleneck.\n\nSo: possible, yes. {"timestamp":"2026-04-22T18:32:53.408Z","type":"event_msg","payload":{"type":"agent_message","message":"Yes, I do think there may be a more fundamental setup issue. I just would not jump straight to “the MLP is too small.”\n\nMy read is:\n\n- More training time alone is probably not the main issue.\n- The runs are not failing because they are too short in a simple sense.\n- They are repeatedly reaching the same regime and then not consolidating it.\n\nThat usually points to a mismatch between the problem and the training setup. In your case, the most suspicious parts are:\n\n- Observation limits\n  `max_depth=3`, `neighbours_depth=3` may just be too local once congestion gets dense. The policy may not see enough of the downstream consequences to coordinate 14-20 trains well.\n\n- Reward / credit assignment\n  The reward is still mostly local progress plus deadlock punishment. That is often too weak for “take a short-term hit to avoid a future network jam” behavior.\n\n- Release regime\n  Releasing all trains at once may make late buckets qualitatively different from earlier ones. That can turn the last curriculum steps into a different problem, not just a harder version of the same one.\n\n- PPO update efficiency\n  Your training loop is fairly conservative and sample-hungry. That can make adaptation on hard coordination regimes too slow.\n\n- Architecture\n  Possible, but I would frame it more as “the architecture may not represent the right information flow,” not just “it needs more hidden units.”\n\nSo yes: I do think the broader setup may be the issue.\n\nWhat I do not think:\n- I do not think the evidence says “just train much longer and it will probably work.”\n- I do not think “keep changing curriculum thresholds” is the answer.\n- I do not think net width alone is the first thing to blame.\n\nIf I were being blunt: stage-5/6 looks like the point where your current formulation stops scaling. That is a setup problem more than a patience problem.\n\nThe best next experiments are probably:\n1. Train directly on env `5` or env `5/6` only.\n2. Increase update frequency / sample efficiency.\n3. Try a simple release cap on harder envs.\n4. Only then test a larger architecture.\n\nThat will tell you whether the real bottleneck is curriculum, optimization, or representation.","phase":"final_answer","memory_citation":null}}
